items.q: Is an embedded system just a small general-purpose computer?
items.correct: No - an embedded system runs dedicated software for one fixed function inside a device; you cannot install new applications on it like a PC.
items.misconception: Yes - an embedded system can run any program you install.
items.random_wrong: Yes - embedded just means the computer is screwed into a case.
items.why: Smallness is mistaken for the defining feature. The key idea is dedicated purpose: the washing machine controller does one job, exactly as shipped.

items.q: Does doubling the number of cores double a computer's speed?
items.correct: No - software must be written to split work across cores, and many tasks cannot be parallelised, so the gain is usually less than double.
items.misconception: Yes - two cores always means exactly twice the performance.
items.random_wrong: Yes - each extra core doubles speed again automatically.
items.why: The marketing arithmetic is seductive. Performance scales with how parallel the workload is - a strictly sequential task gains almost nothing from extra cores.

items.q: Does the program counter hold the data currently being processed?
items.correct: No - the program counter holds the ADDRESS of the next instruction to fetch; data being processed sits in registers such as the accumulator and MDR.
items.misconception: Yes - the program counter stores whatever the CPU is working on.
items.random_wrong: Yes - the program counter counts how many programs are open.
items.why: Every register blurs into 'CPU storage'. Give each its job: PC points ahead, MAR addresses memory, MDR carries data, the accumulator holds results.

items.correct: A computer built into a larger device to perform a dedicated function
items.misconception: Any laptop used at a desk
items.why: It's A computer built into a larger device to perform a dedicated function. “Any laptop used at a desk” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: The address of the memory location currently being accessed
items.misconception: The result of the last calculation
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The address of the memory location currently being accessed. “The result of the last calculation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which three factors most directly affect CPU performance?
items.correct: Clock speed, number of cores and cache size
items.misconception: Screen size, keyboard layout and mouse speed
items.random_wrong: Case colour, fan noise and cable length
items.why: Correct answer: Clock speed, number of cores and cache size. “Screen size, keyboard layout and mouse speed” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Store the results of calculations carried out by the ALU
items.misconception: Hold the address of the next instruction
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Store the results of calculations carried out by the ALU. “Hold the address of the next instruction” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many stages are there in the fetch-decode-execute cycle?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 stages. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
